title: Urgent: Acme Connectors SDK auth failures — SLA & escalation
preview:
From: support@acme-connectors.com
To: camila.reyes@redwood.com
Cc: nina.garcia@acme-connectors.com
Date: 2026-04-10T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Integration: OAuth token failures in SDK v1.3 — urgent

Hi Camila,

We're seeing repeated 401s from Redwood's hosted endpoints when our customers use the Acme Connector SDK v1.3. The failures began around 08:50 PDT and affect 18% of calls to /v1/generate with short prompts. We attached a trimmed log (acme-sdk-logs-20260410.txt) and a sample request/response.

Symptoms: token exchange succeeds, subsequent requests fail mid-stream with "invalid_token" and no retry indicated. Rolling back to v1.2 removes the error. No config changes on our side.

This is blocking our production customers and we've had two outage alerts. We need a triage call and clarity on SLA response times — our contract references a 2-hour response for P1. Please advise next steps and availability for a bridge.

Thanks,
Nina Garcia
Integration Lead, Acme Connectors
attachments: acme-sdk-logs-20260410.txt


From: camila.reyes@redwood.com
To: support@acme-connectors.com
Cc: jonas.weber@redwood.com, marcus.lin@redwood.com, vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com
Date: 2026-04-10T09:28:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration: OAuth token failures in SDK v1.3 — urgent

Hi Nina — thanks for the quick report and the logs. I'm sorry you're seeing this. I've opened a maintenance/incident ticket (MA-8342) and attached the initial ticket PDF for your records.

Immediate next steps I propose:
- 09:45 PDT bridge (I'll host) — please confirm attendees and bridge link
- Jonas and Marcus from infra will look at token validation and rate-limiter traces; Marcus, can you pull token-cache metrics for 08:30–09:30?
- We'll treat this as a P1 for SLA purposes and commit to initial response within 2 hours; need to confirm full-resolution ETA on the bridge.

Temporary mitigations you can try before the call: force-refresh tokens every request (bad for perf but isolates cache issues) and point the SDK at the fallback model variant (model=babbage-compatible) if your workload allows.

I'll host the bridge and loop in Partnerships oncall. Attaching MA ticket: redwood-ma-ticket-8342.pdf.

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Partner Solutions, Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0162


From: jonas.weber@redwood.com
To: camila.reyes@redwood.com
Cc: marcus.lin@redwood.com, support@acme-connectors.com
Date: 2026-04-10T09:41:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration: OAuth token failures in SDK v1.3 — urgent

On Fri, Apr 10, 2026 at 09:28 AM Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> I'll host the bridge and loop in Partnerships oncall. Attaching MA ticket: redwood-ma-ticket-8342.pdf.

Setting up the bridge now. Initial read of the logs suggests an edge-level auth proxy is dropping the refresh handshake under sustained short-lived sessions — likely a race around cache invalidation in the pool. Marcus, please dump auth-proxy/trace for the 08:45 interval and check redis TTLs for token objects.

Camila: from an SLA perspective, I recommend we offer Acme a formal 2-hour response and a 24-hour remediation window for a fix or a formal rollback plan. If remediation requires a patch to the runtime we should offer a temporary capacity workaround (route Acme customers to a pre-warmed pool) to avoid customer impact.

I've escalated to partnerships_oncall@redwood.com and eng-incident. Bridge is scheduled at 09:45 PDT — details below.

Bridge: Zoom link (hosted by Camila): https://zoom.us/j/6150000000
Incident ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INT-1572
Regards,
Jonas Weber
Head of Platform Reliability, Redwood Inference


From: nina.garcia@acme-connectors.com
To: camila.reyes@redwood.com
Cc: support@acme-connectors.com, jonas.weber@redwood.com
Date: 2026-04-12T11:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Integration: OAuth token failures in SDK v1.3 — urgent

Hi Camila and team — thanks for the fast turnaround and for hosting the bridge on Friday. Engineering dug into the patch Jonas mentioned and we've validated a candidate fix in our staging cluster. We've confirmed the behavior is resolved when the token cache key contains request.id instead of session.id (see attached patch-1572.diff).

Acme accepts Redwood's proposed SLA alignment: 2-hour initial response for P1s and a committed 24-hour remediation window when a code change is required. We'll reflect that in our partnership playbook.

Next steps for both sides:
- Redwood: coordinate runtime release window and provide a rollback plan for 2 hours post-release
- Acme: run limited Canary with 10% traffic for 4 hours then escalate to 100% if stable
- Both: update integration README (we'll open a PR) and add the token-key note to the SDK migration guide

Links: hubspot record - https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/company/ACME-8342
Attachments: patch-1572.diff

Appreciate the partnership — Nina
Nina Garcia
Integration Lead, Acme Connectors
support@acme-connectors.com

title: Network flow telemetry requirements for on-prem private deployment
preview:
From: Emma Sullivan <emma.sullivan@edgegrid.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: network-ops@edgegrid.com
Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for yesterday's call. As we start moving toward the private deployment PoC, Network Ops asked for a concrete list of flow telemetry and packet/connection metadata the Redwood private stack exposes from the tenant VPCs and on-prem fabric.

Specifically, we need to validate that: 
- flow logs include L4 tuple + process/instance id (where available)
- egress/ingress NAT translations are recorded
- latency histograms for per-flow forwarding decision time are available
- retention and deletion controls map to our compliance window (90 days)

I've attached our internal spec (edgegrid-flow-specs-v1.pdf) and copied the ops alias. Can you point to the Terraform/Helm modules we should review and what evidence you can provide from CI to satisfy our auditors?

Best,
Emma Sullivan
Head of Network Engineering
EdgeGrid Systems

---
Attachment: edgegrid-flow-specs-v1.pdf (application/pdf, 312 KB)

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Emma Sullivan <emma.sullivan@edgegrid.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Thanks Emma — good, concrete asks. Looping in Markus (runtime networking) and Neha (compliance/iactest owner).

Quick summary of what we can provide out of the box for private (VPC/on-prem) deployments:

- Flow logs: per-tenant flow logs contain 5-tuple + flow duration + bytes/packets. For on-prem we rely on the dataplane agent to emit process and container metadata when the agent runs as privileged.
- NAT visibility: the egress proxy records pre/post NAT 5-tuples in an annotated log stream (we can toggle this in helm values).
- Latency histograms: runtime exposes per-hop forwarding time; we aggregate into 1ms buckets and push to Prometheus histograms.
- Retention controls: logs are routed to the customer sink (S3/MinIO) with lifecycle policies; our private helm chart includes lifecycle CRD mapping for retention TTLs.

What I'd like to do next: 
1) Share a short diff of the Terraform modules and the helm values patch we propose for the on-prem agent so your auditors can see exact changes.
2) Run a CI artifact (iactest) that produces a reproducible evidence bundle (flow samples + histogram exports + lifecycle policy snapshot).

I'll attach a patch with the proposed tf-module changes and a simple diagram. Markus/Neha — can you confirm the iactest checklist we should run (sampling rate, synthetic flows, retention validation)?

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Product Manager, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference

---
Attachments: tf-module-diff.patch (text/x-patch), flow-telemetry-diagram.png (image/png, 48 KB)

From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Emma Sullivan <emma.sullivan@edgegrid.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2026 15:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Emma/Ava — quick technical notes after reviewing the patch. I'm pasting the important parts inline for visibility.

1) Terraform: the proposed module adds an optional `flow_agent_enabled` flag and an `agent_privileged` boolean. We should avoid enabling `agent_privileged` by default; instead, we provide a documented manual step for customers who require process-level attribution (EdgeGrid falls into that category).

2) Helm values: `telemetry.sample_rate` defaults to 0.01; for audit evidence we should run at 1.0 (full capture) in the iactest job. We'll include an `iactest_values.yaml` that sets `telemetry.sample_rate: 1.0`, `log_format: annotated`, and `retention_policy_ttl_days: 90`.

3) CI evidence: I'll add a pipeline stage `iactest/flow-evidence` which will:
   - deploy the agent helm chart with iactest_values.yaml
   - generate synthetic flows (tcp/udp/http) across NAT and hairpin scenarios
   - collect sample logs and a prometheus snapshot (histograms)
   - create a signed evidence bundle (sha256) and upload to the Drive folder Ava linked

Neha — for the evidence bundle to be acceptable, please confirm if a signed SHA256 and the pipeline run logs are sufficient, or if we need a notarized timestamping step.

Markus
Senior Software Engineer, Runtime Networking
Redwood Inference

---
Attachment referenced: iactest_values.yaml (to be added to the repo)


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Emma Sullivan <emma.sullivan@edgegrid.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 9 Apr 2026 14:07:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Thanks — this looks good. From a compliance perspective: 

- Evidence bundle must include: the iactest pipeline run id, signed artifacts (sha256), the prometheus snapshot file, and a config snapshot (helm values + terraform module versions).
- Retention: customer-configured TTL of 90 days is fine; we will include a short runbook snippet in the evidence bundle that shows how to change/delete data and confirm deletion.
- Timestamping: a notarized timestamp would be nice-to-have but not mandatory for the initial audit. A signed pipeline artifact + CI logs that show the run timestamp is acceptable.

Action items (next steps):
- Markus: create the `iactest/flow-evidence` pipeline job and add `iactest_values.yaml` to the repo.
- Ava: we'll send EdgeGrid a one-off evidence bundle after running the job in the staging on-prem lab.
- Emma: confirm any additional artefacts your auditors want (e.g., passenger_process_id mapping).

Neha Kapoor
Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

---
Quoted message included below:
> On Thu, Apr 8, 2026 at 11:03 AM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Thanks Emma — good, concrete asks. Looping in Markus (runtime networking) and Neha (compliance/iactest owner).
> > ...

From: Emma Sullivan <emma.sullivan@edgegrid.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 9 Apr 2026 15:01:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Perfect — this covers what we need for the SOC-analyst walkthrough next week. Two quick follow-ups: 

1) We'll provide a small set of hostnames and test VMs for the synthetic flow run; expect an invite from network-ops.
2) Can you include the `tf-module-diff.patch` in the PR so our procurement team can see the exact resource changes? They use that to validate policy scoping.

If you want, my team can run an initial smoke pass once you have the iactest job ready and share a short findings summary — that might speed up the auditor review.

Thanks again,
Emma

Emma/Ava — quick technical notes after reviewing the patch. I'm pasting the important parts inline for visibility.

1) Terraform: the proposed module adds an optional `flow_agent_enabled` flag and an `agent_privileged` boolean. We should avoid enabling `agent_privileged` by default; instead, we provide a documented manual step for customers who require process-level attribution (EdgeGrid falls into that category).

2) Helm values: `telemetry.sample_rate` defaults to 0.01; for audit evidence we should run at 1.0 (full capture) in the iactest job. We'll include an `iactest_values.yaml` that sets `telemetry.sample_rate: 1.0`, `log_format: annotated`, and `retention_policy_ttl_days: 90`.

3) CI evidence: I'll add a pipeline stage `iactest/flow-evidence` which will:
   - deploy the agent helm chart with iactest_values.yaml
   - generate synthetic flows (tcp/udp/http) across NAT and hairpin scenarios
   - collect sample logs and a prometheus snapshot (histograms)
   - create a signed evidence bundle (sha256) and upload to the Drive folder Ava linked

Neha — for the evidence bundle to be acceptable, please confirm if a signed SHA256 and the pipeline run logs are sufficient, or if we need a notarized timestamping step.

Markus
Senior Software Engineer, Runtime Networking
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: Flow telemetry and evidence for on-prem deployment

Thanks — this looks good. From a compliance perspective: 

- Evidence bundle must include: the iactest pipeline run id, signed artifacts (sha256), the prometheus snapshot file, and a config snapshot (helm values + terraform module versions).
- Retention: customer-configured TTL of 90 days is fine; we will include a short runbook snippet in the evidence bundle that shows how to change/delete data and confirm deletion.
- Timestamping: a notarized timestamp would be nice-to-have but not mandatory for the initial audit. A signed pipeline artifact + CI logs that show the run timestamp is acceptable.

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Subject: Request: Product-specialist SE headcount + comp-band exception

Team —

Following the Strategic-Edge discovery last week, the account requires a product-focused SE (Senior level) with deep embeddings/LLM deployment experience to close the deal in Q3. This is a time-sensitive win: they want a technical partner embedded during POC and have signaled they will consolidate with the vendor that can staff a dedicated SE and run two-week production load tests.

Why this is an exception:
- The role is more senior than our usual SE hire for this team (we need >5 years with on-prem and VPC experience).
- Market comps in SF are pushing total comp ~15–20% above our standard mid-SE band for similar responsibilities.
- The candidate profile is narrow; we expect a 6–8 week sourcing window.

Request:
1) Approval to open 1 headcount (Senior Product Specialist, SE) on the Solutions team (new FTE).
2) One-time comp-band exception: increase offer ceiling to the upper bound of our Senior SE band + $10k hiring premium to secure the candidate.
3) Fast-track offer approval workflow with People Ops (Aditya) to allow a final offer within 48 hours of selection.

Attached: JD_SE_Product_Specialist.pdf (role + must-haves) and a preliminary cost sheet.

Proposed timeline: open req today -> shortlist in 3 weeks -> offer by mid-May if possible.

Happy to jump on a quick call if you want more detail.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Director, Solutions Engineering
Redwood Inference

[attachment: JD_SE_Product_Specialist.pdf (pdf)]

- Can we add a one-week \"top-up\" option that we can trigger mid-pilot if we run into higher-than-expected volumes? We'd prefer a short automatic extension window rather than negotiating a contract amendment.

Hi Ava — thanks for the clear notes and the SOW. Short answers up front: yes on a one-week top-up, and we can offer a discounted incremental rate if we get a commitment signal to convert to an annual contract within 30 days after the pilot.

How we typically structure it: the 4-week timebox is a fixed pilot fee that covers baseline provisioning and support. The top-up is treated as a separate one-week add-on with metered charges; for this situation I can propose a 20% discount on the top-up week if Rivermark signs a 12-month commitment (we'd put that in a simple amendment). If you don't convert, the top-up would be billed at standard metered rate.

I'll loop in Marissa (sales lead) to confirm we can approve the 20% concession and Jordan from contracting if we need an amendment template. Also tagging our compliance note: data residency and retention will follow the Private deployment standard in the SOW if required — we can add a one-paragraph addendum.

Proposed next steps:
1) You confirm start date (Apr 12) and opt-in language for the top-up (simple one-line trigger email to a dedicated alias).
2) I put together a short amendment with the 20% top-up rate conditional on a 12-month commitment.
3) We schedule a kickoff and compliance checklist call.

Cheers,
Avery Johnson

title: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel@chroma-labs.com>, Priya Singh <priya@chroma-labs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Apr 2026 16:12:00 -0400
Subject: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Thanks for the time today — useful call. Quick recap and a short set of asks so we can map pricing to your economic targets. Attachment: redwood-unit-econ-template.xlsx (simple spreadsheet to capture inputs).

Recap (short):
- Use case: in-product assistant + search reranking for power users (B2B SaaS).
- Key unknowns we need to verify: monthly token volumes (inference + embeddings), average tokens per interaction, expected peak concurrency, and your budget envelope for inference.

Asks: can you confirm the following by early next week?
1) Current monthly active users (MAU) cadence we should model (e.g., 10k DAU / 180k MAU).
2) Avg tokens per session (approx): prompt tokens + response tokens per call (order of magnitude is fine).
3) Target budget range for inference (monthly): a bracket helps us pick the right offering.
4) Business targets: target $/1M tokens you'd consider acceptable for production, CAC payback goal (months), and gross margin target for the feature.

Short note on unit-econ framing we use (so the numbers below align when you send inputs):
- Cost we show = inference token cost + embedding storage (amortized) + marginal infra. We typically express vendor-level pricing as $ per 1M tokens for generation, and $ per 1k vectors for embeddings.
- Feature-level cost = (avg tokens per call / 1,000,000) * $/1M tokens. Multiply by daily usage to get monthly feature cost.
- CAC payback: based on conversion rate from free->paid and ARPU per paid customer — we'll map sensitivity.

We'll draft a sample pricing bracket after we get your rough token numbers. If you prefer, fill the attached template and send it back; it has the same fields.

Next steps:
- Daniel/Priya: send the rough usage numbers or the filled template by Mon Apr 6.
- We (Redwood): we'll return a 2-tier pricing sketch (hosted entry + dedicated reserve) and a quick feature-level P&L within 48 hours after receiving inputs.
- Optional: we can schedule a 30-min follow-up to walk the spreadsheet.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
mobile: (415) 555-0123

Attachment: redwood-unit-econ-template.xlsx (simple input fields: MAU, avg tokens/session, sessions/user/day, embedding vectors stored, target margin)

From: Daniel Park <daniel@chroma-labs.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Singh <priya@chroma-labs.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Apr 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Ben — thanks, good summary. Quick answers below and I've attached our internal usage estimate (chroma-usage-estimates.csv). High-level:

- MAU to model: 120k (we're projecting ~20k MAU in 6 months, but conservative model = 120k).
- Avg tokens/session: 220 tokens (prompt ~90, response ~130).
- Sessions per MAU/day: 0.18 (heavier for power users but overall this average).
- Embeddings: ~2.4M vectors stored (used for reranking/fuzzy search).
- Peak concurrency: 85 qps at 95th percentile.

Budget range: our procurement says we have a monthly inference budget for pilot of $6k-$15k — depends on latency needs and caching. If early numbers suggest >$15k/month we're likely to pull scope.

Business targets:
- Target $/1M tokens we'd want to hit for production: ideally <= $3 / 1M tokens.
- CAC payback goal: 6-9 months (we're SaaS; faster payback is better).
- Gross margin target on the feature: 60%+ (anything below starts to look risky to product ROI).

We'll fill the template but the CSV should help you run a quick sketch.

—Daniel
Daniel Park
Head of Product, Chroma Labs
---
Attachment: chroma-usage-estimates.csv

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Apr 2026 11:45:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Looping internal notes — forwarding Daniel's reply. Quick takeaways and what I'd draft back:

1) Volume profile: 120k MAU with 0.18 sessions/day and 220 tokens/session => monthly tokens estimate = 120k * 0.18 * 30 * 220 ≈ 142,560,000 tokens/month (≈143M). Embedding storage: 2.4M vectors — treat as fixed monthly cost for now.

2) Chroma's target: <= $3 / 1M tokens and 60%+ gross margin, CAC payback 6-9 months. They have a pilot budget cap ~ $15k/mo.

3) Pricing levers we can offer:
- Hosted entry tier (no commit): $/1M tokens a bit higher but low operational overhead.
- Dedicated reserved commit (3/6/12 mo): material discount if they commit to a baseline.
- Feature-level optimizations: suggest aggressive prefix caching for common prompts, and model quantization (gives ~25-40% cost down in practice).

4) Quick back-of-envelope: if we price at $2.75 / 1M tokens then monthly inference cost = 143 * $2.75 ≈ $393.25 (wait — unit mismatch: that arithmetic is wrong if read literally; should be 143 * $2.75 = $393.25? we need to be explicit in reply). I'll draft the formal calc for Ben to send.

Action: Ben — I'll help draft a simple P&L showing cost per feature and CAC-payback sensitivity. I recommend proposing:
- Pilot hosted: $3.25 / 1M tokens (no commit) to stay within their bandwidth;
- Dedicated 12-month commit: $1.75 / 1M tokens if they commit to >= 120M tokens/month for first quarter.

Marissa
VP, Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Apr 2026 14:50:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Short math I'll paste into the template so we don't have rounding confusion — use these in the final email to Chroma.

Inputs (Daniel):
- MAU = 120,000; sessions/day = 0.18; avg tokens/session = 220; month days = 30 => monthly calls = 120k * 0.18 * 30 = 648,000 calls/month. Monthly tokens = 648k * 220 = 142,560,000 tokens (~142.56M).

Feature-level cost (example):
- If price = $2.75 / 1M tokens => monthly inference cost = 142.56 * 2.75 = $392.54 (142.56 * 2.75 = 392.54). Note: token units = millions.
- Cost per call = (220 / 1,000,000) * $2.75 = $0.000605 per call (~$6.05e-4).
- Cost per DAU-month (assuming 0.18 sessions/day) = 0.18 * 30 * $0.000605 ≈ $0.00327 per MAU per month attributable to that feature.

CAC payback sensitivity (simple):
- If ARPU per paid customer = $25/month and conversion from trial to paid = 2.5%, then lifetime revenue per acquired trial cohort (first month) looks like low; we should model payback on incremental revenue per converted customer.
- Example: assume 1 new paying customer per 40 trials; incremental gross margin target is 60% => we need to ensure CAC / (ARPU * margin) <= payback months goal.

Working example: if CAC = $40 (cost to acquire a customer), ARPU = $25, margin = 60% => monthly gross contribution per customer = 25 * 0.6 = $15. Payback = 40 / 15 ≈ 2.7 months (very healthy). If ARPU is lower or conversion worse, payback rises.

Guidance for Ben's reply to Chroma: show the $/1M bands, convert to monthly totals (as above), and explicitly show cost per feature and cost per MAU so product folks can judge margin impact. Offer a dedicated-reserve discount tier as Marissa suggested and call out optional cost reductions from caching/quantization.

I can draft the P&L table once they return the template. Also propose a 30-min follow-up to walk through the numbers and talk commits. I'll volunteer a rough forecast sheet for 6/12 months.

—Karthik
Director, Platform Economics

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel@chroma-labs.com>, Priya Singh <priya@chroma-labs.com>

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel@chroma-labs.com>, Priya Singh <priya@chroma-labs.com>

Subject: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Thanks for the time today — useful call. Quick recap and a short set of asks so we can map pricing to your economic targets. Attachment: redwood-unit-econ-template.xlsx (simple spreadsheet to capture inputs).

Recap (short):
- Use case: in-product assistant + search reranking for power users (B2B SaaS).
- Key unknowns we need to verify: monthly token volumes (inference + embeddings), average tokens per interaction, expected peak concurrency, and your budget envelope for inference.

Asks: can you confirm the following by early next week?
1) Current monthly active users (MAU) cadence we should model (e.g., 10k DAU / 180k MAU).
2) Avg tokens per session (approx): prompt tokens + response tokens per call (order of magnitude is fine).
3) Target budget range for inference (monthly): a bracket helps us pick the right offering.
4) Business targets: target $/1M tokens you'd consider acceptable for production, CAC payback goal (months), and gross margin target for the feature.

Short note on unit-econ framing we use (so the numbers below align when you send inputs):
- Cost we show = inference token cost + embedding storage (amortized) + marginal infra. We typically express vendor-level pricing as $ per 1M tokens for generation, and $ per 1k vectors for embeddings.
- Feature-level cost = (avg tokens per call / 1,000,000) * $/1M tokens. Multiply by daily usage to get monthly feature cost.
- CAC payback: based on conversion rate from free->paid and ARPU per paid customer — we'll map sensitivity.

We'll draft a sample pricing bracket after we get your rough token numbers. If you prefer, fill the attached template and send it back; it has the same fields.

Next steps:
- Daniel/Priya: send the rough usage numbers or the filled template by Mon Apr 6.
- We (Redwood): we'll return a 2-tier pricing sketch (hosted entry + dedicated reserve) and a quick feature-level P&L within 48 hours after receiving inputs.
- Optional: we can schedule a 30-min follow-up to walk the spreadsheet.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Ben — thanks, good summary. Quick answers below and I've attached our internal usage estimate (chroma-usage-estimates.csv). High-level:

- MAU to model: 120k (we're projecting ~20k MAU in 6 months, but conservative model = 120k).
- Avg tokens/session: 220 tokens (prompt ~90, response ~130).
- Sessions per MAU/day: 0.18 (heavier for power users but overall this average).
- Embeddings: ~2.4M vectors stored (used for reranking/fuzzy search).
- Peak concurrency: 85 qps at 95th percentile.

Budget range: our procurement says we have a monthly inference budget for pilot of $6k-$15k — depends on latency needs and caching. If early numbers suggest >$15k/month we're likely to pull scope.

Business targets:
- Target $/1M tokens we'd want to hit for production: ideally <= $3 / 1M tokens.
- CAC payback goal: 6-9 months (we're SaaS; faster payback is better).
- Gross margin target on the feature: 60%+ (anything below starts to look risky to product ROI).

We'll fill the template but the CSV should help you run a quick sketch.

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>

Date: Sat, 4 Apr 2026 11:45:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Looping internal notes — forwarding Daniel's reply. Quick takeaways and what I'd draft back:

1) Volume profile: 120k MAU with 0.18 sessions/day and 220 tokens/session => monthly tokens estimate = 120k * 0.18 * 30 * 220 ≈ 142,560,000 tokens/month (≈143M). Embedding storage: 2.4M vectors — treat as fixed monthly cost for now.

2) Chroma's target: <= $3 / 1M tokens and 60%+ gross margin, CAC payback 6-9 months. They have a pilot budget cap ~ $15k/mo.

3) Pricing levers we can offer:
- Hosted entry tier (no commit): $/1M tokens a bit higher but low operational overhead.
- Dedicated reserved commit (3/6/12 mo): material discount if they commit to a baseline.
- Feature-level optimizations: suggest aggressive prefix caching for common prompts, and model quantization (gives ~25-40% cost down in practice).

4) Quick back-of-envelope: if we price at $2.75 / 1M tokens then monthly inference cost = 143 * $2.75 ≈ $393.25 (wait — unit mismatch: that arithmetic is wrong if read literally; should be 143 * $2.75 = $393.25? we need to be explicit in reply). I'll draft the formal calc for Ben to send.

Action: Ben — I'll help draft a simple P&L showing cost per feature and CAC-payback sensitivity. I recommend proposing:
- Pilot hosted: $3.25 / 1M tokens (no commit) to stay within their bandwidth;
- Dedicated 12-month commit: $1.75 / 1M tokens if they commit to >= 120M tokens/month for first quarter.

Marissa
VP, Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Apr 2026 14:50:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Post-call: pricing & unit-econ follow-up (tokens, $/1M, CAC payback)

Short math I'll paste into the template so we don't have rounding confusion — use these in the final email to Chroma.

Inputs (Daniel):
- MAU = 120,000; sessions/day = 0.18; avg tokens/session = 220; month days = 30 => monthly calls = 120k * 0.18 * 30 = 648,000 calls/month. Monthly tokens = 648k * 220 = 142,560,000 tokens (~142.56M).

Feature-level cost (example):
- If price = $2.75 / 1M tokens => monthly inference cost = 142.56 * 2.75 = $392.54 (142.56 * 2.75 = 392.54). Note: token units = millions.
- Cost per call = (220 / 1,000,000) * $2.75 = $0.000605 per call (~$6.05e-4).
- Cost per DAU-month (assuming 0.18 sessions/day) = 0.18 * 30 * $0.000605 ≈ $0.00327 per MAU per month attributable to that feature.

CAC payback sensitivity (simple):
- If ARPU per paid customer = $25/month and conversion from trial to paid = 2.5%, then lifetime revenue per acquired trial cohort (first month) looks like low; we should model payback on incremental revenue per converted customer.
- Example: assume 1 new paying customer per 40 trials; incremental gross margin target is 60% => we need to ensure CAC / (ARPU * margin) <= payback months goal.

Working example: if CAC = $40 (cost to acquire a customer), ARPU = $25, margin = 60% => monthly gross contribution per customer = 25 * 0.6 = $15. Payback = 40 / 15 ≈ 2.7 months (very healthy). If ARPU is lower or conversion worse, payback rises.

Guidance for Ben's reply to Chroma: show the $/1M bands, convert to monthly totals (as above), and explicitly show cost per feature and cost per MAU so product folks can judge margin impact. Offer a dedicated-reserve discount tier as Marissa suggested and call out optional cost reductions from caching/quantization.

I can draft the P&L table once they return the template. Also propose a 30-min follow-up to walk through the numbers and talk commits. I'll volunteer a rough forecast sheet for 6/12 months.

—Karthik
Director, Platform Economics

title: Custom bundle options and downgrade path for Fintegral
preview:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena@fintegral.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Fintegral Procurement <procurement@fintegral.com>
Date: Sun, 22 Mar 2026 09:13:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Packaging options for inference (Fintegral)

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team really liked the latency and autoscaling behavior on your hosted tier. We're getting internal pushback around price tiers and whether we can mix features from Dedicated and Hosted. A few specific questions: 

- Can we start on Hosted (pay-as-you-go) and move to Dedicated later without a full migration project? Any data/kv cache considerations?
- Is there a way to bundle the advanced observability (longer retention) with a mid-tier Dedicated plan, or is that an enterprise-only add-on?
- If we downgrade mid-contract (e.g., after month 6), how is proration handled — do you allow switching down a full node pool?

We're trying to decide between a small Dedicated commit or staying on Hosted for another quarter. Can you outline practical upgrade/downgrade paths and a sample cost delta for a 6-12 month horizon?

Thanks,
Elena
Elena Ruiz
Head of Product, Fintegral
elena@fintegral.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

--
Attachment: requirements-summary.pdf (pdf, 74 KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena@fintegral.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 23 Mar 2026 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Packaging options for inference (Fintegral)

Hi Elena — great questions, appreciate the clarity. Short answers up front, then a proposed pragmatic path: 

1) Migration path: Yes — you can start on Hosted and migrate to Dedicated with minimal app changes. The main work is reconfiguring endpoints and validating KV cache warmup for long-running chat sessions; our runtime supports transparent model versioning so model behavior remains consistent. We provide a migration checklist and a cache pre-warm utility that cuts cold-starts by ~60%.

2) Observability bundling: Observability (extended retention/advanced traces) can be included on Dedicated as an add-on to mid-tier plans during a pilot. It's typically packaged as an enterprise add-on, but for pilots we can offer a temporary bundle so your SREs can validate retention and alerting.

3) Downgrade/proration: We support mid-term downgrades with prorated credits toward the remainder of the committed period; moving down a node pool is permitted but subject to minimum commit sizes for reserved instances. For Hosted -> Dedicated moves we prorate the Dedicated commitment start date and backfill any Hosted consumption as credit.

Proposed path (practical, low-friction):
- Q2 (pilot): Stay on Hosted PAYG, enable advanced observability as a temporary add-on for 90 days (no extra commit) so SRE can validate metrics.
- Post-pilot: If ok, sign a 6-12 month Dedicated pilot with a small reserved pool (1–2 node equivalent) + observability bundled. We can offer a pilot discount (see quote).
- Failover: Keep Hosted fallback routes for burst capacity to avoid failed requests during Dedicated maintenance.

I've attached a revised quote with two bundle options and a side-by-side feature matrix. If you'd like, I can schedule a 30-min call with Laura (pricing lead) to run through numbers and proration scenarios.

Next steps: Which option should I prepare a formal quote for? Option A: 6-month Dedicated pilot + observability add-on. Option B: Continue Hosted + observability pilot and revisit at month 3.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.ai
(650) 555-0123

--
Attachments: RDW-quote-v2.pdf, feature-matrix.xlsx

From: Elena Ruiz <elena@fintegral.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Fintegral Procurement <procurement@fintegral.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Mar 2026 08:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Packaging options for inference (Fintegral)

Thanks Ben — that helps. A few follow-ups from procurement and product: 

- Procurement asks if you can do a 10% promotional credit on the first invoice for the Dedicated pilot. They want to use this to offset integration work.
- Product wants to know whether we can opt into quantization support and model variant routing in the mid-tier bundle or whether those are enterprise-only features.
- If we sign a 12-month commit for a small Dedicated pool, can we scale down after month 6 without a penalty (other than proration)? We need explicit language for the SOW.

Could you cc your manager or pricing lead who can confirm we won't hit surprise clauses?

Best,
Elena

--
(One-line reply from Procurement: "10% credit requested — awaiting Redwood confirmation.")

From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Elena Ruiz <elena@fintegral.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 25 Mar 2026 15:48:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Packaging options for inference (Fintegral)

Hi Elena, Ben — looping in pricing/ops. 

Ben, I reviewed RDW-quote-v2 and cross-checked with finance. We can approve a one-time 10% promotional credit on the first invoice for the Dedicated pilot (applies to committed monthly fee, not usage overages). I'll add explicit language to the SOW: 
- Mid-term scale-down allowed after month 6 with prorated credits; minimum reserved size for reduction is one node-equivalent.
- Quantization profiles and model-variant routing are available on our mid-tier Dedicated pilot bundle (no enterprise upgrade required) — exposure to beta kernels may apply.

Elena, I will prepare an updated SOW and Tag the DocuSign envelope for signature. Expected changes: updated billing clause, proration language, and a term that the observability bundle is included for a 90-day evaluation window.

If that works, Ben — please send the updated SOW and I will review for final sign-off.

Thanks,
Laura Bennett

laura@redwood.ai

- A short table of differences (what changes between Dedicated vs Private for routing, KMS, audit logs, and tenancy)

Lauren — thanks for the clear list. We can get a concise package over to you this week. Plan: 

1) I will assemble a 1-page executive summary + a 2-page network diagram (control vs data plane layers)
2) Kimberly will prepare a short matrix that contrasts Dedicated vs Private for KMS, audit logs, and egress controls
3) Monica will export our latest SIG/CAIQ answers and a CAIQ sheet with highlighted responses you flagged on the call

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.inference>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.inference>
Date: Mon, 23 Mar 2026 08:41:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Request: architecture overview + SIG/CAIQ for Dedicated/Private deployments

Ben/Monica — attaching the technical notes I'll include in the packet. High-level points (short bullets we should surface in the one-pager):

- Control plane vs data plane: private deployments run a customer-managed control plane (VPC peering / private endpoint) when requested; Dedicated uses a segregated control plane managed by Redwood with tenant fencing at the infra and network layers.
- KV/caching and model runtime: KV cache and model state are isolated per-tenant in both modes; Dedicated provides physically reserved GPU pools, Private can be configured behind customer-managed VPCs with our sidecar egress proxies.
- Egress and DLP: Outbound traffic default-deny; explicit egress rules for telemetry and artifact stores. For Private we support customer-controlled egress via NAT/Gateway and on-prem routing.
- KMS: Dedicated integrates with Redwood-managed KMS (customer keys supported); Private supports customer-managed KMS (BYOK) and HSM integration for higher assurance.
- Logs & Audit: Tenant-scoped audit logs are available in all modes; Private can stream logs to customer SIEM via secure forwarder.

Ask: I need a clear statement from Product/Infra on the maximum shared blast radius in Dedicated (we should quantify what is shared vs dedicated: control plane hosts, telemetry collectors). Karthik — can you provide the short quant? I drafted the network diagram and attached as PDF.

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_network_diagram_v1.pdf

Kimberly Park
Senior Security Architect, Redwood Inference

Subject: RE: Request: architecture overview + SIG/CAIQ for Dedicated/Private deployments

Ben/Monica — attaching the technical notes I'll include in the packet. High-level points (short bullets we should surface in the one-pager):

- Control plane vs data plane: private deployments run a customer-managed control plane (VPC peering / private endpoint) when requested; Dedicated uses a segregated control plane managed by Redwood with tenant fencing at the infra and network layers.
- KV/caching and model runtime: KV cache and model state are isolated per-tenant in both modes; Dedicated provides physically reserved GPU pools, Private can be configured behind customer-managed VPCs with our sidecar egress proxies.
- Egress and DLP: Outbound traffic default-deny; explicit egress rules for telemetry and artifact stores. For Private we support customer-controlled egress via NAT/Gateway and on-prem routing.
- KMS: Dedicated integrates with Redwood-managed KMS (customer keys supported); Private supports customer-managed KMS (BYOK) and HSM integration for higher assurance.
- Logs & Audit: Tenant-scoped audit logs are available in all modes; Private can stream logs to customer SIEM via secure forwarder.

Ask: I need a clear statement from Product/Infra on the maximum shared blast radius in Dedicated (we should quantify what is shared vs dedicated: control plane hosts, telemetry collectors). Karthik — can you provide the short quant? I drafted the network diagram and attached as PDF.

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_network_diagram_v1.pdf

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jared Lee <jared.lee@acme.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 18 Mar 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal

Hi Jared —

Thanks for the call earlier. Per our conversation I attached an alternative pricing pack that re-configures capacity, support tiers, and a migration credit so you can compare an apples-to-apples replacement plan against the incumbent. High level:

- Option A (price-competitive): 12-month committed plan, 20% off our published hosted rates, standard SLAs, 2-week migration credits for onboarding.
- Option B (feature-preserve): Maintain current rate, include dedicated onboarding team + two migration credits (up to 160 GPU-hours) and an SLO addendum for availability.
- Option C (pilot-first): 3-month pilot at a reduced token price with the option to convert to a committed contract with a deeper discount.

I've attached redwood_alternative_pricing.xlsx — it breaks the per-token math, expected throughput bands, and estimated migration effort. Can you confirm which option the procurement team prefers and whether you can share the GigaServe quote line items (capacity, discounts, and any credits) so we can align? Happy to set up 30 minutes to walk through.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood_alternative_pricing.xlsx (xlsx)]

Cc: Acme Procurement <procurement@acme.com>

Subject: Re: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal

Hey Ben —

Thanks for the fast turnaround. We reviewed the spreadsheet — helpful. Procurement flagged that GigaServe (the incumbent) has sent a commercial counter-offer that is materially lower on per-token costs if we accept their 24-month lock. They attached their official quote; I forwarded it to procurement.

Key asks from our side: can Redwood match the headline per-token number GigaServe is advertising, or can you propose a meaningful migration credit + transition SLA that makes a short-term delta acceptable? Our procurement lead also needs an itemized side-by-side on feature parity (KV cache, private deployment options, audit logs).

Attached: gigaserve_competitor_quote.pdf
Procurement timeline: award scheduled week of Mar 29. We need best and final by EOD Mar 24 to feed the internal scoring matrix.

-Jared
Jared Lee
Director, Platform Engineering — Acme Corporation
[Attachment: gigaserve_competitor_quote.pdf (pdf)]


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 08:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal (internal assessment)

Ben / Sergio —

Quick internal run on the GigaServe quote Jared shared. Walkthrough notes and recommended plays below. I attached a focused migration_cost_comparison.xlsx that isolates the first-year economics with and without migration credits.

Summary of levers we can use to be competitive without eroding long-term value:

1) Term + commitment: offer a 12-month committed plan at a 20% headline discount (Option A) — this is our baseline price-match posture.
2) Support & credits: add one-time migration credits (up to 120 GPU-hours) + a 4-week dedicated onboarding window. This addresses the transition friction line item GigaServe is discounting away.
3) Feature carve: preserve core features (KV cache, private deploy) but offer a temporary quantization guidance package (engineering hours) as an alternative to hardware discounts.
4) SLA trade: where necessary, shift to a 99.5% availability SLO for price-match deals, keep 99.9% for full-price enterprise deals.

Costs & risk: to make the math work we should:
- Require minimum committed spend (monthly floor) to avoid abuse.
- Limit the migration credit window to 90 days post-signature.

Recommendation: respond with Option A (12-month, 20% off list + 120 GPU migration credit + 4-week onboarding + optional SLO at 99.5%). This preserves margins, gives procurement a tangible transition concession, and keeps the door open to upsell technical services later.

If leadership signs off I think we can send a revised term sheet + DocuSign link. Sergio — any quick thoughts on whether engineering can guarantee the 4-week onboarding with the credit assumptions?

Thanks,
Marissa
Solutions Lead — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: migration_cost_comparison.xlsx (xlsx)]

Subject: Re: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal

Jared —

Appreciate the transparency and the timeline. After an internal review we've approved the recommended posture from Marissa with a narrow optimization to help you meet procurement deadlines. Our best-and-final:

- Headline: 12-month committed plan at 18% off published hosted rates (slightly deeper than the internal baseline).
- Migration: One-time migration credit of 150 GPU-hours (to help shorten cutover), valid for 90 days post-signature.
- Onboarding: 4-week dedicated onboarding + priority routing for first 60 days.
- SLA: 99.5% availability SLO on the matched price (we can include a limited SLO uplift clause if you convert to a 24-month term).

Subject: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal

Hi Jared —

Thanks for the call earlier. Per our conversation I attached an alternative pricing pack that re-configures capacity, support tiers, and a migration credit so you can compare an apples-to-apples replacement plan against the incumbent. High level:

- Option A (price-competitive): 12-month committed plan, 20% off our published hosted rates, standard SLAs, 2-week migration credits for onboarding.
- Option B (feature-preserve): Maintain current rate, include dedicated onboarding team + two migration credits (up to 160 GPU-hours) and an SLO addendum for availability.
- Option C (pilot-first): 3-month pilot at a reduced token price with the option to convert to a committed contract with a deeper discount.

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 08:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Vendor replacement cost scenarios & pricing — Redwood proposal (internal assessment)

Ben / Sergio —

Quick internal run on the GigaServe quote Jared shared. Walkthrough notes and recommended plays below. I attached a focused migration_cost_comparison.xlsx that isolates the first-year economics with and without migration credits.

Summary of levers we can use to be competitive without eroding long-term value:

1) Term + commitment: offer a 12-month committed plan at a 20% headline discount (Option A) — this is our baseline price-match posture.
2) Support & credits: add one-time migration credits (up to 120 GPU-hours) + a 4-week dedicated onboarding window. This addresses the transition friction line item GigaServe is discounting away.
3) Feature carve: preserve core features (KV cache, private deploy) but offer a temporary quantization guidance package (engineering hours) as an alternative to hardware discounts.
4) SLA trade: where necessary, shift to a 99.5% availability SLO for price-match deals, keep 99.9% for full-price enterprise deals.

Costs & risk: to make the math work we should:
- Require minimum committed spend (monthly floor) to avoid abuse.
- Limit the migration credit window to 90 days post-signature.

Recommendation: respond with Option A (12-month, 20% off list + 120 GPU migration credit + 4-week onboarding + optional SLO at 99.5%). This preserves margins, gives procurement a tangible transition concession, and keeps the door open to upsell technical services later.

If leadership signs off I think we can send a revised term sheet + DocuSign link. Sergio — any quick thoughts on whether engineering can guarantee the 4-week onboarding with the credit assumptions?

Thanks,
Marissa

title: CareLink - 429 surge after tier migration
preview:
From: Tom Ellis <t.ellis@carelink.health>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: CareLink Ops <ops@carelink.health>
Date: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 07:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Production errors — 429 flood since last night

Ava / team,

We're seeing a sharp spike in 429s starting ~01:30 UTC this morning after we moved a large cohort of traffic to the new enterprise tier. Impact: API calls from our ingestion pipeline are being rate-limited, causing downstream processing delays and timeouts for clinical syncs.

Quick details:
- Affected endpoints: /v1/generate and /v1/embeddings

title: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal
preview:
From: Samira Khan <samira@helixhealth.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Leung <tom.leung@helixhealth.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 09:23:00 -0700
Subject: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Hi Avery,

Quick note from our side — we are seeing a sharper-than-expected uptick in inference volume for our clinical triage flows and need to add ~30% additional token capacity mid-cycle. We would like to: 

- Add capacity now to avoid throttling for the next 6 weeks
- Push the added capacity onto our standing annual contract so it co-terms at renewal in September

Can you share pricing options (prorated invoice vs. voucher/credit approach) and any uplift discounts for mid-cycle expansion? Also, what happens to the per-token price if we annualize the add vs. buying a short-term burst?

We'd like something that minimizes paperwork and keeps our finance team happy — Tom is looped in to coordinate PO/invoice timing.

Thanks,
Samira
Samira Khan
Head of Platform Partnerships
Helix Health



From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira@helixhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Hi Samira — thanks for the heads up and congrats on the growth. Short answer: we have two pragmatic paths that customers use depending on procurement preference. 

Option A — Prorated add + invoice now:
- Add 30% capacity immediately, prorated for the remainder of your current term (6 months)
- One-time invoice for the prorated amount; existing per-token pricing stays the same
- At renewal we can convert the add into annual committed capacity with a prospective discount applied to the added portion only

Option B — Voucher/annualize now (co-term immediately):
- Convert the requested add into an annualized capacity add and issue an adjusted invoice/PO that co-terms with your September renewal
- Pricing for the annualized add gets a mid-contract uplift discount (we typically offer a 7–12% range depending on baseline usage and commitment)

Attached is a quick pro-forma showing the math both ways. Ben, can you confirm the baseline usage numbers I used and any edge-case notes on bucket carryover? Priyom, I left a comment where finance needs to confirm invoice timing and whether a voucher would trigger different tax handling.

If you prefer, we can do the voucher route and issue a single DocuSign amendment to minimize PO churn.

Cheers,
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555-0123

Attachment: helix_topup_proforma.xlsx (spreadsheet — capacity, proration, two-path comparison)



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Samira Khan <samira@helixhealth.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 13:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Samira + team,

I pulled the baseline and simulated the two options (attached PDF becomes the executive summary). A few notes: 

- Baseline used: 1.2B tokens/month (average over trailing 90 days). A 30% uplift implies ~360M incremental tokens/month.
- Option A (prorate): one-time prorated invoice of ~0.15x your monthly charge (because the add runs for ~6 months). No change to per-token unit price. Works well if procurement wants minimal contract changes.
- Option B (annualize/co-term): if you annualize the add now, we can apply a 9% discount to the incremental annualized bucket. That yields ~6% net savings vs. paying prorated month-to-month across the next 12 months.

Edge-case: switching from Option A to B mid-term is possible but requires an amendment and the incremental discount will be pro-rated based on the remaining term. If you plan to expand further in the next 90 days, voucher + co-term is cleaner operationally.

I've attached a short redwood_pricing_summary_v3.pdf with the figures and a proposed amendment line item that legal can accept with minimal redlines.

Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood_pricing_summary_v3.pdf (summary, discount calc, amendment language)



From: Samira Khan <samira@helixhealth.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Leung <tom.leung@helixhealth.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Thanks — this is helpful. A couple of follow-ups from finance and ops: 

1) If we go with Option B (annualize/co-term + 9% on the added portion), can you confirm the invoicing cadence? Tom's team prefers a single invoice now that certifies the change for our records.
2) For the amendment: how long does it typically take for legal to sign? We want minimal back-and-forth; if the amendment language is short we can fast-track it.
3) SLA ask: given we are increasing traffic to a critical path, would Redwood consider a temporary 99.9% latency SLO credit for the added bucket for the first 90 days in case we see regressions during ramp? Not a permanent change, just a temporary safety measure.

If we can get confirmed numbers and a DocuSign link today, I can push to get PO approved this week.

Best,
Samira



From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira@helixhealth.com>, Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Hi Samira — looping in finance confirmation and the DocuSign link. See answers inline: 

1) Invoicing cadence: we can issue a single pro-forma invoice that covers the annualized add and ties to the existing contract. That invoice would be dated today and apply against your PO; bookkeeping will reflect the change on your September co-term.

2) Amendment turnaround: our legal team uses the one-line amendment Ben included in the PDF for these cases. With your approval we can route the amendment through DocuSign — typical signature turnaround is 24–48 hours for customers using e-sign.

3) SLA credit: we can offer a temporary credit structure — up to 2% credit on that incremental bucket if latency SLOs are missed for >2 consecutive days during the first 90-day ramp. This is limited, non-recurring, and tied only to the added capacity. If that works, I'll add the SLA credit clause to the amendment.

DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/DocuSign?envelopeId=0a1b2c3d4e

Subject: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Hi Avery,

Quick note from our side — we are seeing a sharper-than-expected uptick in inference volume for our clinical triage flows and need to add ~30% additional token capacity mid-cycle. We would like to: 

- Add capacity now to avoid throttling for the next 6 weeks
- Push the added capacity onto our standing annual contract so it co-terms at renewal in September

Can you share pricing options (prorated invoice vs. voucher/credit approach) and any uplift discounts for mid-cycle expansion? Also, what happens to the per-token price if we annualize the add vs. buying a short-term burst?

Date: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Hi Samira — thanks for the heads up and congrats on the growth. Short answer: we have two pragmatic paths that customers use depending on procurement preference. 

Option A — Prorated add + invoice now:
- Add 30% capacity immediately, prorated for the remainder of your current term (6 months)
- One-time invoice for the prorated amount; existing per-token pricing stays the same
- At renewal we can convert the add into annual committed capacity with a prospective discount applied to the added portion only

Option B — Voucher/annualize now (co-term immediately):
- Convert the requested add into an annualized capacity add and issue an adjusted invoice/PO that co-terms with your September renewal
- Pricing for the annualized add gets a mid-contract uplift discount (we typically offer a 7–12% range depending on baseline usage and commitment)

Attached is a quick pro-forma showing the math both ways. Ben, can you confirm the baseline usage numbers I used and any edge-case notes on bucket carryover? Priyom, I left a comment where finance needs to confirm invoice timing and whether a voucher would trigger different tax handling.

If you prefer, we can do the voucher route and issue a single DocuSign amendment to minimize PO churn.

Cheers,
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555-0123

Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 13:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Mid-cycle capacity add + alignment to annual renewal

Samira + team,

I pulled the baseline and simulated the two options (attached PDF becomes the executive summary). A few notes: 

- Baseline used: 1.2B tokens/month (average over trailing 90 days). A 30% uplift implies ~360M incremental tokens/month.
- Option A (prorate): one-time prorated invoice of ~0.15x your monthly charge (because the add runs for ~6 months). No change to per-token unit price. Works well if procurement wants minimal contract changes.
- Option B (annualize/co-term): if you annualize the add now, we can apply a 9% discount to the incremental annualized bucket. That yields ~6% net savings vs. paying prorated month-to-month across the next 12 months.

Edge-case: switching from Option A to B mid-term is possible but requires an amendment and the incremental discount will be pro-rated based on the remaining term. If you plan to expand further in the next 90 days, voucher + co-term is cleaner operationally.

I've attached a short redwood_pricing_summary_v3.pdf with the figures and a proposed amendment line item that legal can accept with minimal redlines.

Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Paul Nguyen <p.nguyen@fastcrm.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@fastcrm.com>
Date: Wed, 18 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Rate card + minimum monthly commit for hosted API

Hi Ben,

Quick intro — I'm Paul (head of Infra at FastCRM). We're evaluating Redwood's hosted API for powering a new AI-assistant feature. Procurement asked for your published rate card and clarification on whether you have a minimum monthly spend or commit options for the hosted (non-dedicated) offering.

Two quick asks:
- Can you share baseline pricing (per-token / embedding) and the smallest available committed credit package?
- What discounted tiers exist if we commit to a monthly spend (12-month or 6-month)? Any constraints on roll-over credits or how unused credits are handled?

We are starting with an estimate of ~250M tokens/month across mixed generation and embeddings. We don't need or want Dedicated capacity right now — just want predictable pricing and the option to convert to Dedicated later.

Attachment: fastcrm_ratecard_request.pdf (stub) — includes our usage profile.

Would appreciate a short options matrix we can include in our procurement pack.

Thanks,
Paul
Paul Nguyen
Head of Infrastructure, FastCRM
paul.nguyen@fastcrm.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Paul Nguyen <p.nguyen@fastcrm.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 18 Mar 2026 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Rate card + minimum monthly commit for hosted API

Paul — thanks for the note and the usage profile. Below is a concise options matrix for hosted API commits (non-dedicated). I put conservative estimates and the practical policies so procurement can attach to the SOW. Happy to jump on a 20–30m call to walk through.

Summary (hosted API, no Dedicated):
- Pay-as-you-go (no commit): standard list rates (generation / embeddings). Good for pilots; less predictable spend.
- Committed credit packages (monthly invoice or prepay): provide tiered discounts against list, applied as credits to invoices. Credits reduce per-token effective cost.

Typical committed options we offer:
- Starter: $10,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 10% off list. Includes 30-day trial credit for evaluation. Credits do not roll forward beyond invoice month unless explicitly added to a custom contract.
- Growth: $25,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 15% off list. Eligible for 1 complimentary Technical Onboarding session and up to 7-day credit roll policy (one-time carryover).
- Scale: $50,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 20% off list. Includes access to enhanced support and 60-day limited credit rollover for occasional spikes.
- Enterprise: $150,000+/month (negotiated). Discount: 25–35% (sliding based on term and case-by-case). Includes SLA add-on options, compliance bundle, and negotiation on retention.

Other important notes:
- Commits are billed monthly as credits. Unused credits are partially refundable on annual contracts with 90-day notice (requires enterprise term).
- Commitments can be applied to Hosted API only; if you later move to Dedicated we typically provide credit transfer discussions but that requires a ramp plan.
- SLA, data retention, and longer-term log retention are add-ons (priced separately).

I've attached our up-to-date rate card: 2026_redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf (stub).

If 250M tokens/month is your target, the Growth or Scale tiers typically are the closest fit — I can run a quick token-cost estimate to show absolute $/month after discount.

Next steps: I can send a formal proposal with three options (Starter/Growth/Scale) and a DocuSign ready SOW if you want to move quickly.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: Rate card + minimum monthly commit for hosted API

Paul — thanks for the note and the usage profile. Below is a concise options matrix for hosted API commits (non-dedicated). I put conservative estimates and the practical policies so procurement can attach to the SOW. Happy to jump on a 20–30m call to walk through.

Summary (hosted API, no Dedicated):
- Pay-as-you-go (no commit): standard list rates (generation / embeddings). Good for pilots; less predictable spend.
- Committed credit packages (monthly invoice or prepay): provide tiered discounts against list, applied as credits to invoices. Credits reduce per-token effective cost.

Typical committed options we offer:
- Starter: $10,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 10% off list. Includes 30-day trial credit for evaluation. Credits do not roll forward beyond invoice month unless explicitly added to a custom contract.
- Growth: $25,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 15% off list. Eligible for 1 complimentary Technical Onboarding session and up to 7-day credit roll policy (one-time carryover).
- Scale: $50,000/month commit (12-month). Discount: 20% off list. Includes access to enhanced support and 60-day limited credit rollover for occasional spikes.
- Enterprise: $150,000+/month (negotiated). Discount: 25–35% (sliding based on term and case-by-case). Includes SLA add-on options, compliance bundle, and negotiation on retention.

Other important notes:
- Commits are billed monthly as credits. Unused credits are partially refundable on annual contracts with 90-day notice (requires enterprise term).
- Commitments can be applied to Hosted API only; if you later move to Dedicated we typically provide credit transfer discussions but that requires a ramp plan.
- SLA, data retention, and longer-term log retention are add-ons (priced separately).

I've attached our up-to-date rate card: 2026_redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf (stub).

If 250M tokens/month is your target, the Growth or Scale tiers typically are the closest fit — I can run a quick token-cost estimate to show absolute $/month after discount.

Next steps: I can send a formal proposal with three options (Starter/Growth/Scale) and a DocuSign ready SOW if you want to move quickly.

title: POC Onboarding deliverables and technical inventory (API keys, org setup, allowlist, peering)
preview:
From: Alex Gomez <alex@cardiotech.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@cardiotech.io
Date: 2026-03-15T09:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Intro + POC plan (next steps)

Thanks Ben — excited to move forward. The exec team wants the evaluation to be scope-limited: 2 weeks, host a small integration that touches embeddings and chat. Before we start, our infra lead asked for a short checklist of what you need from our side to avoid delays (API keys, org onboarding, IP allowlist, and any peering work). Can you send the list and a rough gating timeline so procurement and infra can align?

Also attaching an internal note on our security review cadence (will share SOC2 copy once procurement asks).

—Alex
CardioTech, Head of Product

Attachment: internal-security-notes.txt (text)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Gomez <alex@cardiotech.io>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-03-15T11:22:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Intro + POC plan (next steps)

Alex — great, thanks. Below is a compact onboarding deliverables list and our recommended gating timeline to hit a 2-week evaluation window. I kept items explicit so procurement/infra can act in parallel.

POC onboarding deliverables (what we need from CardioTech):
1) Org setup: point us at an org owner (email) so we can provision the trial tenant and invite two CardioTech seats (dev + infra). We’ll send a short SSO invite if you want SAML.
2) API keys: confirm whether you want hosted API (Redwood cloud) or private (VPC). For hosted — we generate tenant API keys and you rotate them into your env. For private — we’ll need a service account and key exchange process.
3) IP allowlist: list of egress IPs from your environment that need to be allowed (and a CIDR if using peered VPC rules). We’ll provide Redwood’s endpoint IPs and a short-lived fingerprint for initial testing.
4) VPC peering / network: if you prefer peering, share a primary VPC ID and contact for peering ops (we’ll publish a minimal peering template). Note peering adds ~3 business days depending on cloud firewall approvals.
5) Acceptance checklist & test prompts: a short set of prompts (3-5) and an embedding query to validate results and latency SLOs.

Gating timeline (recommended, optimistic):
- Day 0: Org owner & contact confirmed; Redwood shares MAP v1 (attached) and temporary test keys.
- Day 1: CardioTech returns IP allowlist + VPC info if peering.
- Day 2: Redwood provisions tenant & issues test keys; infra begins connectivity tests.
- Day 3–4: Integration and basic smoke tests (embedding similarity, chat latency).
- Day 5: Meet for initial readout; decide if additional perf tuning or move to private deployment.

Notes and risks:
- If you choose VPC peering, add ~2–4 business days for network approvals.
- For private deployments we’ll need an offline KMS handoff for keys; that adds time but is manageable within the eval if initiated early.

I attached a short MAP draft that maps these items to owners and dates. Karthik, Neha, Olga — tagging you for technical owners and any additions.

Next steps: Alex — can you confirm org owner and whether CardioTech prefers hosted API or peering by EOD tomorrow? Procurement copied for visibility.

Thanks,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachment: MAP-CardioTech-v1.pdf (PDF)


From: Olga Petrov <olga@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Gomez <alex@cardiotech.io>, Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-03-16T08:35:00-07:00
Subject: RE: RE: Intro + POC plan (network details)

Alex — quick technical clarifications from the infra side so you can check with your network team: 

1) Hosted API path (fastest):
   - We will provision a tenant and a pair of API keys (read/write test keys + ephemeral admin).
   - You only need to add our public egress IPs to your allowlist; I’ll send an allowlist file with CIDRs and a short-lived TLS fingerprint for initial TLS pinning if required.
   - No VPC configuration required.

2) VPC peering option (preferred for private data):
   - We need your VPC ID, region, and a peering contact. We’ll generate a CloudFormation/Terraform peering template.
   - Required firewall rules: allow outbound 443 to our peered subnet and permit return traffic.
   - Peering acceptance typically takes 1–3 business days once both networks approve.

3) API key lifecycle and rotation:
   - Test keys expire in 14 days by default. For longer trials we can extend. Production keys require KMS integration; we’ll share a KMS prep checklist.

title: Dedicated renewal: capacity planning for new model mix and reranker
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated renewal: capacity planning for new model mix and reranker

Jordan —

Our renewal for the Dedicated contract is coming up on 2026-04-01. Thanks for the heads-up on the product roadmap change — you mentioned plans to shift some traffic to the new 70B model with a 32k context window and to add a reranker service that will fan out calls per request. That combination materially changes GPU and memory needs.

To move quickly, can you confirm the following by EOD Wednesday?
- Target go-live for the 70B + reranker: (date)
- Expected steady-state concurrent QPS for generation requests (not tokens) and expected peak QPS
- Typical average and 99th percentile request lengths (tokens)
- Whether reranker will run as an independent call (additional model invocation) or be pipelined in your stack

Initial thought: we can either add a separate reserved pool sized for the reranker and 70B endpoints (recommended for isolation), or scale your existing pool and adjust QoS. I attached a lightweight capacity_plan_v1.xlsx with two rough configurations (isolation vs pooled).

If you can share the QPS/latency targets and procurement timeline, I’ll loop in pricing and a formal quote.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0199

Attachment: capacity_plan_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@acme.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Sana Patel <sana.patel@acme.ai>, Aaron Lee <aaron.lee@acme.ai>
Date: Wed, 11 Mar 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated renewal: capacity planning for new model mix and reranker

Thanks Ben — quick answers below.

- Target go-live: 2026-04-15 for a controlled rollout (25% first week, ramp to 100% over 4 weeks)
- Steady-state peak generation QPS (expected): 110 qps peak across US region; steady-state 65 qps
- Avg request length: 220 tokens; P99 request length: 1,900 tokens (we have a long-tail batch of large summaries)
- Reranker: separate downstream call per candidate set (avg 5 candidates) — so roughly +5x model invocations per user query for the reranker endpoint

Procurement: Sana (cc’d) will handle purchase order. Timeline: need final pricing and contract by 2026-03-20 to meet internal approval cycles.

We’re sensitive to latency; ideally holding p50 generation under 120ms and p95 under 400ms. What does that imply for reserved GPUs and throughput guarantees?

—Jordan
Jordan Ellis
Director of Product, ACME.ai
jordan.ellis@acme.ai

On Tue, Mar 10, 2026 at 09:12 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Jordan —
> 
> Our renewal for the Dedicated contract is coming up on 2026-04-01. Thanks for the heads-up on the product roadmap change — you mentioned plans to shift some traffic to the new 70B model with a 32k context window and to add a reranker service that will fan out calls per request....

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 08:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated renewal: capacity planning for new model mix and reranker

Jordan / Sana —

Appreciate the details. Based on 110 qps peak and the reranker multiplier, we ran the workload through our sizing model and produced two recommended configurations with pricing. High level: the 32k context and reranker increase VRAM pressure and concurrency needs, so we recommend isolating the reranker on its own reserved pool and pinning the 70B generation to a quantized instance class.

Option A (recommended, fewer total GPUs, better latency):
- 8x A100-80GB reserved (pinned pool) dedicated to 70B quantized model + reranker pool for isolation
- Throughput commitment: 95% of baseline peak with on-demand burst capacity for 20% overcommit
- Latency target: p50 < 110ms (generation), p95 < 380ms (subject to request length distribution)
- Monthly price (post 12-month commit): $178,500 / month (quote attached)

Option B (cost-conservative, higher concurrency):
- 16x T4-equivalent reserved (pooled) — more parallelism but higher tail latency on 32k windows
- No burst capacity; recommended for teams that prioritize cost over strict p95 latency
- Monthly price (post 12-month commit): $132,000 / month

We can include a 10% commit discount if you sign a 12-month reserved capacity contract at renewal. I’ve attached redwood_dedicated_quote_v3.pdf and an updated capacity_plan_v2.xlsx that shows how the reranker traffic is isolated to a separate pool and the expected token throughput.

Next steps: if you prefer Option A we’ll prepare a DocuSign with the specific reserved pool sizes and the SLA addendum. Vivek, can you confirm the maximum allowable SLA credit in renewals for a deal in this band?

Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Attachment: redwood_dedicated_quote_v3.pdf (PDF)
Attachment: capacity_plan_v2.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Ellis <jordan.ellis@acme.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Mar 2026 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated renewal: capacity planning for new model mix and reranker

Jordan — Ben —

Ops notes from a staging and rollout perspective:
- We can stand up an isolated reranker pool (Option A) using quantized 70B instances; that avoids interference with your gen endpoints and keeps KV cache policies tight.
- Migration plan: we suggest a 4-week ramp (25/50/75/100) with a smoke test at each step. During ramp we’ll enable prefix caching for prompts under 512 tokens to reduce redundant work.
- Important: we’ll need a representative test corpus and a runbook for rollback (I attached a short migration_runbook.md stub). Please include 1–2 weeks of synthetic traffic that mirrors your 1.9k token tail to validate p95.
- For the reranker, set a max-candidates limit to 5 per query initially and use batching window 15ms — this keeps GPU efficiency high without harming p50 latency too much.

I’ll coordinate the staging playbook and monitoring dashboards (trace hooks for per-route token counts). Also recommend a canary that pins 10% of traffic to the non-quantized 70B for quality comparison during week 1.

Marissa Cole
Senior Customer Reliability Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

Jordan / Sana —

Appreciate the details. Based on 110 qps peak and the reranker multiplier, we ran the workload through our sizing model and produced two recommended configurations with pricing. High level: the 32k context and reranker increase VRAM pressure and concurrency needs, so we recommend isolating the reranker on its own reserved pool and pinning the 70B generation to a quantized instance class.

Option A (recommended, fewer total GPUs, better latency):
- 8x A100-80GB reserved (pinned pool) dedicated to 70B quantized model + reranker pool for isolation
- Throughput commitment: 95% of baseline peak with on-demand burst capacity for 20% overcommit
- Latency target: p50 < 110ms (generation), p95 < 380ms (subject to request length distribution)
- Monthly price (post 12-month commit): $178,500 / month (quote attached)

Option B (cost-conservative, higher concurrency):
- 16x T4-equivalent reserved (pooled) — more parallelism but higher tail latency on 32k windows
- No burst capacity; recommended for teams that prioritize cost over strict p95 latency
- Monthly price (post 12-month commit): $132,000 / month

We can include a 10% commit discount if you sign a 12-month reserved capacity contract at renewal. I’ve attached redwood_dedicated_quote_v3.pdf and an updated capacity_plan_v2.xlsx that shows how the reranker traffic is isolated to a separate pool and the expected token throughput.
