title: SaaSFlow: slab pricing simulation & commit phasing
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 14:22:00 -0700
Subject: SaaSFlow: slab pricing simulation & commit phasing

Ethan —

Thanks again for the deep dive today. Per our conversation I attached a couple of slab simulations so you can see the unit economics across different commit shapes. The attached slab_simulation_v2.xlsx includes:
- Monthly token bands (0-500M, 500M-2B, 2B+)
- Per-token rate at each band and effective blended rates for 3 commit sizes
- A model for 6-month ramp (0% month 1 -> 100% month 6) and a 12-month steady commit

Key assumptions called out in the sheet: throughput smoothing, auto-burst cap (25%), and prefix cache savings (estimated 12%).

A few things to highlight up front:
- We can offer a slab discount starting at 15% off hosted baseline for a ~1B token/mo commit.
- For multi-month phasing we commonly do a weighted effective credit that preserves margin while acknowledging ramp risk (I included a sample 'phased credit' column).
- Rollover credits are available for unused committed tokens within the quarter (not unlimited).

Can you confirm the monthly token target you expect at steady state and whether your finance team requires invoice-level credits or line-item adjustments? If you want, I can run a simulation using your actual last-12-month token profile — and Ben (pricing) and Laura (finance) are cc'd to help finalize.

Next step: if you confirm the steady-state target and preferred phasing (6 vs 12 months), I'll pull a term-sheet + phased slab proposal.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com | +1 415 555 0192
Redwood Inference

Attachment: slab_simulation_v2.xlsx (Excel worksheet)


From: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: SaaSFlow Procurement <procurements@saasflow.io>
Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 17:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SaaSFlow: slab pricing simulation & commit phasing

Aditya —

Thanks for sending this over. A few clarifying items from our side:
- Steady-state target: ~1.2B tokens/month (after ramp).
- Preferred ramp: 6 months (we expect ~20% month-over-month growth to reach steady-state).
- Rollover: we need at least partial rollover for unused committed tokens between months within the quarter (Finance prefers line-item credits on the next invoice).
- Billing cadence: prefer monthly invoices with an explicit credit line; open to Net30.

Would also like to see a version of the simulation where the slab is phased (e.g., lower initial minimum with step-ups) and one where we get an annual prepay discount option. Can Ben and Laura loop in suggested legal/billing language for credits?

Ethan
Ethan Walters
Head of Platform, SaaSFlow
ethan@saasflow.io | +1 206 555 0148

Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 17:47:00 -0700

Perfect — thanks for the details. Looping Ben and Laura in as requested.

Ben / Laura — Ethan confirmed a target of ~1.2B tokens/mo with a 6-month ramp (expect ~20% MoM). Requested outputs:
- Phased slab simulation (start lower, step-up bands over 6 months).
- An annual prepay option with an additional 6% off the blended rate.
- Rollover language that results in invoice-level credits (partial rollover within the quarter).

Ben: can you run the phased slab and attach a tidy proposal (numbers + a short note on margin impact)? Laura: can you flag any billing/legal constraints for invoice-level credits vs. post-invoice adjustments?

I told Ethan we'll aim to have a polished term sheet + phased slab by early next week.

Thanks,
Aditya



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Mar 2026 09:11:00 -0700
Subject: Phased slab proposal for SaaSFlow (RD-2026-2319)

Ethan / Aditya —

Attached is the phased_slab_proposal.pdf with two commercial options. High level:
Option A (phased commit, monthly billing):
- Months 1-2: committed 400M tokens/month, rate $0.00018/token
- Months 3-4: committed 800M tokens/month, rate $0.00015/token
- Months 5-6+: committed 1.2B tokens/month, rate $0.00013/token
- Effective blended rate over 6 months (assuming your 20% MoM ramp): $0.000148/token
- Rollover: unused committed tokens may be credited on next invoice within the quarter (credit applied as line-item).

Option B (annual prepay):
- Single 12-month prepay for expected 1.2B tokens/mo at $0.000122/token (6% additional discount vs Option A blended)
- No monthly rollover (prepay model replaces rollover)

Margin note: Option A preserves flexibility; Option B gives best unit economics but increases revenue recognition complexity and requires prepay PO.

If this aligns, we can convert Option A to a term sheet with explicit credit calculations and sample invoice formatting.

Attachment: phased_slab_proposal.pdf

—Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Pricing, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com



From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>, SaaSFlow Procurement <procurements@saasflow.io>
Date: Sat, 14 Mar 2026 10:32:00 -0700
Subject: Billing & terms considerations for phased commit (SaaSFlow)

Team / Ethan —

A few quick finance/legal items to note before finalizing the term sheet:
- Billing cadence: Net30 standard. If SaaSFlow requests Net45, we need C-level approval for increased days outstanding.
- Credits vs adjustments: We can do invoice-level credits for rollover within the quarter. Credits will appear as negative line-items and will be applied to the following invoice. We cannot do retroactive refund checks beyond the quarter without senior approval.
- PO & prepay: For Option B (prepay), procurement must issue a PO and we require an internal revenue ops ticket to handle prepayment accounting.
- SOW / signature: attached a draft SOW with sample language for committal and rollback clauses. Please review and mark up.

Attachment: SaaSFlow-SOW-draft.docx

I'll take the lead on the SOW redline once the commercial option is chosen.

Best,
Laura Bennett
Director, Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
laura_bennett@redwood.com



From: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: SaaSFlow Procurement <procurements@saasflow.io>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Mar 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SaaSFlow: slab pricing simulation & commit phasing

All —

Thanks for the clear options. We prefer Option A (phased commit) with the invoice-level rollover credits as you described. We will:
- Issue a PO for the initial two-month minimum (400M tokens/mo) to start the ramp.
- Accept Net30.
- Request the term sheet + SOW redline so our legal can review; aim to sign next week.

Can you send a concise term sheet (one page) that summarizes: option selected, monthly commit steps, rates, rollover mechanics, and billing cadence? Also propose a 30-minute signature readiness call tomorrow or Tuesday so we can expedite.

Ethan

title: Exec update: Runtime cost optimization — workload-aware batching defaults (SLO tiers) | status + go/no-go gates
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Mar 12, 2026 at 9:18 AM PDT
Subject: Exec update: Runtime cost optimization — workload-aware batching defaults (SLO tiers) | status + go/no-go gates

Sharing a leadership-level status update on “workload-aware batching defaults” (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers) ahead of the runtime-1.22 cut.

TL;DR
- On track for staged canary in late March, with a hosted subset rollout target early April if gates stay green.
- Expected unit economics impact: meaningful cost reduction for standard + cost-efficient tiers without breaking published latency SLOs.
- Primary risks we’re actively mitigating: fast-tier tail latency regression on one model family, and telemetry cardinality / cost.

Progress (last 2 weeks)
1) Runtime + config plumbing
- Tiered batching profile schema is in (backwards compatible; existing routes keep behavior unless tier defaults enabled).
- Scheduler now consumes workload signals: streaming vs non-streaming + prompt-length buckets. Concurrency signal is in progress behind a flag.
- Dedicated override precedence implemented (customer override > route policy > tier default).

2) Observability + explainability
- Tier label is present on the key latency/cost metrics, with cardinality controls (after early canary tests showed the obvious foot-gun).
- Reason codes emitted for “why this profile” (tier default vs override vs model constraint vs overload mode).

3) Benchmarking + perf canary
- Mixed-workload scenarios (mixed prompt lengths + mixed streaming) are now in benchmark-lab. Perf canary gates updated to enforce tier-specific p95/p99 thresholds.

Expected impact (current perf summary)
- Standard tier: ~8–12% better throughput (tokens/sec per GPU) on representative hosted workloads; p95 within target in the canary harness. $/1K tokens improvement tracks throughput.
- Cost-efficient tier: ~15–22% throughput improvement in non-streaming + higher concurrency mixes; requires tighter overload guardrails to avoid queue blowups.
- Fast tier: intended to be neutral on cost (minor throughput impact acceptable) with stronger p99 protections. This is where we saw a p99 regression on one model family in one region under mixed workloads; mitigations below.

Key risks + mitigations
1) Fast tier tail latency regressions (p99)
- Root cause hypothesis: admission control + wait-budget parameters interacting poorly with long-prompt prefill under mixed traffic.
- Mitigation: fast-tier anti-starvation tweaks + more conservative max-wait + stricter queue admission when long-prefill dominance detected.
- Status: fix is implemented behind a flag; re-running benchmark-lab + perf canary baselines this week.

2) Overload behavior + queue instability (cost-efficient)
- Risk: aggressive batching can increase queue depth quickly during bursts.
- Mitigation: overload mode with backpressure + max queue depth limits; hard stop to load shed before tail latency explodes.

3) Telemetry cost / cardinality
- Risk surfaced in early internal canary simulation; tier label can amplify series count.
- Mitigation: cardinality controls + tier sampling rules; additional “hotfix” path is ready if we see series explosion again.

Proposed rollout (subject to go/no-go gates)
- Internal canary: 1 region, 1–2 top model families, fast + standard enabled first; cost-efficient only on non-streaming endpoints.
- Hosted API subset: 5% traffic for standard; fast opt-in for streaming routes; cost-efficient opt-in first.
- Dedicated: opt-in only initially; provide clear override precedence docs + support playbook.

Go/No-Go gates (what must be green)
1) Perf canary
- Fast tier: p99 regression <= 3% vs baseline on top models/regions; no starvation under mixed workloads.
- Standard tier: p95 regression <= 2% vs baseline; p99 regression <= 3%.
- Cost-efficient tier: p95 allowed up to tier target, but no queue blowups (queue depth bounded; no sustained overload mode in steady state).

2) Reliability + error budget
- No increase in 5xx / timeouts attributable to batching changes in canary.
- Error budget burn within weekly guardrails during canary; paging thresholds for fast-tier p99 only (standard/cost-efficient are ticketing).

3) Observability
- Tier-labeled dashboards live + validated; reason codes present in traces for sample traffic.
- Telemetry series count within budget (explicit cap by region/model/tier).

4) Rollback readiness
- Kill switch verified end-to-end (runtime flag + control plane) with a documented rollback runbook.

Attachments
- ExecUpdate_WorkloadAwareBatchingDefaults_2026-03-12.pdf (1-pager)
- TieredBatching_GoNoGo_Gates_v3.xlsx (gates + owners)

If helpful, I can do a 15-min walkthrough in next week’s product/eng leadership sync.

—
Ava Chen
Program Lead, Runtime Cost Optimization
Redwood Inference


On Thu, Mar 12, 2026 at 10:02 AM PDT, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Mar 12, 2026 at 10:02 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Exec update: Runtime cost optimization — workload-aware batching defaults (SLO tiers) | status + go/no-go gates

Thanks Ava.

Two asks from the Optimize side so we don’t over-promise:
1) Can we add an explicit note that “cost-efficient” is primarily targeted at non-streaming workloads (at least for initial launch), and that streaming endpoints should default to standard unless the customer opts into fast?
2) For the exec 1-pager: do we have a single “headline number” for hosted unit cost reduction we’re comfortable with (even as a range) so Product can align messaging?

Also +1 on the 15-min walkthrough. I’ll slot it if you send me 2–3 times.

Aditya


On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 8:26 AM PDT, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 8:26 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Exec update: Runtime cost optimization — workload-aware batching defaults (SLO tiers) | status + go/no-go gates

+1, this is good.

Hosted API rollout considerations from my side:
- I’d like the initial hosted subset to be “standard only” by default (5% ramp) and keep fast and cost-efficient as explicit opt-ins until we have at least 1 week of canary data.
- We should define an explicit customer comms trigger for fast-tier p99 regression (status page vs support proactive outreach). Even if we don’t expect it, we should decide now.

Question: are we comfortable tying the go/no-go to “no sustained overload mode in steady state” as a gate? That seems a little subjective—can we define it as e.g. <1% of requests entering overload mode over a 60-min window during canary?

Nikhil


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 11:06 AM PDT
Subject: Re: Exec update: Runtime cost optimization — workload-aware batching defaults (SLO tiers) | status + go/no-go gates

All makes sense.

Aditya
1) Yes — we’ll state clearly: cost-efficient is initially targeted to non-streaming, high concurrency workloads. For streaming, we’ll recommend standard by default; fast is the “stable streaming latency” option.
2) Headline number: for hosted, we should stick to a range and the tier qualifier. Proposed: “Standard: 8–12% $/1K token reduction on representative workloads; Cost-efficient: 15–22% on non-streaming high concurrency workloads; Fast: cost neutral / slight increase acceptable to protect p99.” I’ll update the 1-pager wording to match.

Nikhil
- Agree on standard-only default for the first hosted subset.
- For overload-mode gate: yes, we’ll make it quantitative. I’ll align with SRE on a crisp definition; your suggestion is a good starting point. Tentatively:
  - Overload mode entry rate < 1% over rolling 60 min during canary, and
  - No continuous overload mode > 5 min on a single cluster without triggering automatic shed + alert.
- Customer comms trigger: I’ll propose a simple matrix for status page vs proactive outreach based on fast-tier p99 delta + duration + impacted traffic share; will share for review.

I’ll send 3 times for a 15-min walkthrough; aiming for Tue/Wed next week.

—
Ava

Ava Chen
Program Lead, Runtime Cost Optimization
Redwood Inference

title: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Megan Li <megan.li@aurorafin.com>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@aurorafin.com>
Cc: procurement@aurorafin.com
Date: Mon, 09 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist

Hi Megan / Oliver,

Thanks for the detailed RFI — attached is our first-pass assimilation roadmap and a lightweight interoperability acceptance checklist to help Aurora align internal teams. Key highlights:

- Proposed 6-week onboarding cadence (pre-prod shadowing -> staged canary -> full cutover). See tab \"Milestones\" in the attached roadmap.
- Canary plan: 3-week ramp with percent-based traffic shifts (1%, 5%, 20%, 60%, 100%) and telemetry gates at each step.
- Integration touchpoints: VPC peering (or PrivateLink), SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning, and mutual TLS for service-to-service calls.
- Data controls: model version pinning, per-route token redaction, and optional prefix caching to limit PII exposure.

Items we need from Aurora to finalize timeline:
1) Preferred canary windows (UTC range) and maintenance blackout days
2) VPC CIDR for peering/testing and NAT/Egress constraints
3) Decision on SSO vs API key for service accounts (we support both)
4) Any runbook owners for Aurora (name/email) so we can include them in the kick-off

Attachments included: redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (timeline + gating), integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (acceptance checklist), network_requirements_summary.pdf (ports & endpoints).

If helpful, we can run a 30-minute walkthrough of the worksheet tomorrow (Tue). Proposed times: 10:00–10:30am PT or 2:00–2:30pm PT.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

---
Attachments:
- redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet, 5 sheets)
- integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (PDF, 4 pages)
